children.title: Self-Coherent Multisymbol Detection of Optical Differential Phase-Shift Keying
children.metadata: self-coherent detection; decision feedback; optical preprocessing; electronic postprocessing; DPSK; multivector modulation; coherent communication; optical communication; phase modulation; integrated-optoelectronic receiver
children.content: Moshe Nazarathy, Senior Member, IEEE, Xiang Liu, Senior Member, IEEE, Louis Christen, Member IEEE, Yannick K. Lize, Student Member, IEEE, Student Member, OSA, and Alan E. Willner, Fellow, IEEE, Fellow, OSA<|txt_split|>Abstract—This paper treats the recently emerging self-coherent multivector decision-feedback-aided detection techniques for optoelectronic devices. The goal of this work is to investigate both an optical preprocessing approach and an electronic postprocessing approach. We present in detail (i) the generation of a synthetic low-phase-noise effective local oscillator out of the modulation of the electric field, and (ii) the application of the hybrid (i) and (ii) the role of polypeptide (interleaved) DPSK in the optical preprocessing approach in easing the decision feedback realization process. In particular, we show that the proposed method can be used for general \(M_{\mathrm{f}}\) and differential quadrature phase-shift keying in particular; iv) three alternative integrated-optoelectronic receiver implementations for the optical preprocessing approach detailing the different processing strategies, including digital and electronic analog and digital building blocks; and (v) the principle, and alternative implementations, of the digital postprocessing approach to self-coherent detection. We finally compare the capabilities of the self-coherent detection and digital coherent detection techniques.<|txt_split|>Index Terms-Coherent communication, decision feedback, multiplexing, multiplexing of optical and multivisional PSK, optical communication, phase modulation.

It would be advantageous to develop differential equations that are more accurate than those obtained from the use of coherent detection, yet with substantially lower complexity, marginally exceeding that of conventional differential phase-shift keying (DPSK) [1]. We have recently introduced several novel detection architectures, referred to here as self-coherent, approaching coherent performance [2]-[6] yet incurs implementation complexity between that of a full coherent and a single coherent detector. In addition, we have also addressed front-ends entailing delay interferometers (Ds) with multiple delays. Furthermore, the use of analog circuits in the proposed approach has shown the potential to compensate for transmitter, receiver, and channel impairments, thereby behaving in a similar manner to the conventional analog circuit.<|txt_split|>The self-coherent (SC) terminology indicates that an accurate phase reference is synthesized from the signal itself without additionally requiring a physical phase-locked optical local oscillator (LO) light source. Remarkably, the new designs approach the sensitivity advantage of coherent detection while simultaneously reducing the noise level. The high-quality synthetic LO is extracted from the received optical signal by averaging over prior symbols that are randomly modulated, yielding a beneficial phase-noise reduction effect through a linear combination of these previous symbols. The key for synthesizing an improved LO is to remove the data modulation on the past symbols through decision feedback (DF), e.g., by controlling the DIs in the optical preprocessing stage. In order to achieve this, we have developed a technique consisting of along with graphical and mathematical descriptions of the key process of aligning earlier complex symbols at discrete times \(k = 2, k = 3, k = 4,\ldots\) which participate in synthesizing an effective LO (improved phase reference) along with the previous symbol at time \(k = 1\). Data removal or phase realignment is performed by means of complex rotations using decision feedback, such that the rotated symbols become collinear with, and reinforce, the reference symbol at time \(k = 1\) as indicated. To achieve this, we have developed a technique consisting of applied phase reference with reduced phase noise, applied to the "self-coherent" detection of the current symbol at time \(k\).<|txt_split|>Notably, the SC principle pertains to differential phase shiftkeyed transmission over a window comprising multiple \((>2)\) symbol intervals, referred to as chips (a "chip" being the regular band interval over which the phase of the transmitted optical signal stays constant). Similar multisymbol DPSK (MS- DPSK) [12] decision-feedback-aided multisymbol phase estimation techniques [13]-[15] have been earlier considered in the wireless communication domain; however, their porting to a different channel is nontrivial, essentially due to the availability of phase information in the square-law detection process. Note that some special forms of MS-DPSK were realized without DF [16]-[18], albeit at the expense of implementation complexity.<|txt_split|>The DF SC MS-DSPKS alternative optical receiver structures are presented in Figure 1. The structure is shown in Fig. 2, and the differently abstracted wireless receiver structures [13]-[15].<|txt_split|>However, the processing required in the wake of square-law detection, the various optical and electronic realizations, and the interpretation of the improved reference phase estimation as self-coherent gain are novel contributions.<|txt_split|>Two main SC system architectures (in turn partitioned into subarchitectures) have emerged, respectively corresponding to preprocessing in the optical domain versus postprocessing in the electronic (digital) domain. The electronic postprocessing systems introduced in [7]–[11] under the name multisymbol phase control (MSPC) and the digital system introduced in [12]–[14] under the DfS) but require heavier electronic high-speed DF processing, either analog [7]–[9] or digital [11]. In contrast, the optical preprocessing architectures introduced in [2]–[4] under the name multichip/ DPSK (MC-DPSK) require more complex DI devices accomplishing most of the DF processing directly in the optical domain while reducing the complexity of the DfS in the electronic domain (which nevertheless is still somewhat more expensive than for conventional DPSK). A related recursive SC detection system was introduced in [19] based on a recirculating delay line.<|txt_split|>This paper is mainly devoted to nonrecursive optical preprocessing realizations of the self-coherent principle. Synthetic LO generation is interpreted in terms of estimation theory as a technique for estimating the phase shift keying (DIPPSK) and its contribution by an averaging effect over multiple symbols. The brief differential binary phase-shift keying (DIBPSK) treatments in [2]-[6] are extended to the more complex differential quadrature phase-shift keying (DQPSK) and, more generally, M-array phase-shift keying (M-MSK). In this work, we discuss processing SC architectures and their tradeoffs are compared. In Section II, we review a "polyphase" interleaved version of conventional DPSK. The SC principle is explained in Section III, rigorously modeling the attainable self-coherent gain, i.e., the reduction of phase noise power entailed in the process of synchrotronics. We also present some results on the performance of polyphase operation on esching the DF requirements and provides an overview of the alternative integrated-optoelectronic realizations, which are detailed and compared in Sections VI and VII. For completeness, the electronic postprocessing approach is briefly discussed in Section VIII. The final section concludes with Section IX. Finally, we conclude that the self-coherent versus the digital coherent detection techniques.

children.content: The image illustrates the principle of operation of self-coherent detection, divided into three parts: (a) DSPSK (Differential Phase Shift Keying) differential phase detection, (b) coherent detection, and (c) novel self-coherent detection. In part (a), the received signal is processed through a DSPSK demodulator to extract phase differences, while part (b) shows coherent detection involving local oscillator generation and phase comparison. Part (c) presents a novel self-coherent detection method that uses a self-coherent phase recovery block to process the received signal, avoiding the need for a separate local oscillator, with a block diagram showing phase recovery and signal processing steps.

subnode.title: I. INTRODUCTION_3
subnode.metadata: self-coherent detection; phase reference synthesis; decision feedback; phase noise reduction; analog circuit compensation; synthetic LO; complex symbol alignment; optical postprocessing; coherent detection advantage; reduced noise level
subnode.content: Y. K. Lize is with ITF Laboratories, Montreal, QC H4N 2G7, Canada (e-mail: yklyize@itflabs.com).Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/JLT.2007.912055analog [7]-[9] or digital [9]-[11] electronic postprocessing, can also be characterized as self-coherent. Furthermore, the use of analog circuits in the proposed approach has shown the potential to compensate for transmitter, receiver, and channel impairments, thereby behaving in a similar manner to the conventional analog circuit.The self-coherent (SC) terminology indicates that an accurate phase reference is synthesized from the signal itself without additionally requiring a physical phase-locked optical local oscillator (LO) light source. Remarkably, the new designs approach the sensitivity advantage of coherent detection while simultaneously reducing the noise level. The high-quality synthetic LO is extracted from the received optical signal by averaging over prior symbols that are randomly modulated, yielding a beneficial phase-noise reduction effect through a linear combination of these previous symbols. The key for synthesizing an improved LO is to remove the data modulation on the past symbols through decision feedback (DF), e.g., by controlling the DIs in the optical preprocessing stage. In order to achieve this, we have developed a technique consisting of along with graphical and mathematical descriptions of the key process of aligning earlier complex symbols at discrete times \(k = 2, k = 3, k = 4,\ldots\) which participate in synthesizing an effective LO (improved phase reference) along with the previous symbol at time \(k = 1\). Data removal or phase realignment is performed by means of complex rotations using decision feedback, such that the rotated symbols become collinear with, and reinforce, the reference symbol at time \(k = 1\) as indicated. To achieve this, we have developed a technique consisting of applied phase reference with reduced phase noise, applied to the "self-coherent" detection of the current symbol at time \(k\).

subnode.title: I. INTRODUCTION_4
subnode.metadata: SC principle; differential phase shiftkeyed transmission; multisymbol DPSK; decision-feedback-aided; phase estimation; square-law detection; implementation complexity
subnode.content: Notably, the SC principle pertains to differential phase shiftkeyed transmission over a window comprising multiple \((>2)\) symbol intervals, referred to as chips (a "chip" being the regular band interval over which the phase of the transmitted optical signal stays constant). Similar multisymbol DPSK (MS- DPSK) [12] decision-feedback-aided multisymbol phase estimation techniques [13]-[15] have been earlier considered in the wireless communication domain; however, their porting to a different channel is nontrivial, essentially due to the availability of phase information in the square-law detection process. Note that some special forms of MS-DPSK were realized without DF [16]-[18], albeit at the expense of implementation complexity.

The structure is shown in Fig. 2, and the differently abstracted wireless receiver structures [13]-[15].However, the processing required in the wake of square-law detection, the various optical and electronic realizations, and the interpretation of the improved reference phase estimation as self-coherent gain are novel contributions.Two main SC system architectures (in turn partitioned into subarchitectures) have emerged, respectively corresponding to preprocessing in the optical domain versus postprocessing in the electronic (digital) domain. The electronic postprocessing systems introduced in [7]–[11] under the name multisymbol phase control (MSPC) and the digital system introduced in [12]–[14] under the DfS) but require heavier electronic high-speed DF processing, either analog [7]–[9] or digital [11]. In contrast, the optical preprocessing architectures introduced in [2]–[4] under the name multichip/ DPSK (MC-DPSK) require more complex DI devices accomplishing most of the DF processing directly in the optical domain while reducing the complexity of the DfS in the electronic domain (which nevertheless is still somewhat more expensive than for conventional DPSK). A related recursive SC detection system was introduced in [19] based on a recirculating delay line.

subnode.title: I. INTRODUCTION_6
subnode.metadata: self-coherent principle; synthetic LO generation; differential phase-shift keying; M-MSK; polyphase interleaved processing; phase noise reduction; integrated-optoelectronic realizations; electronic postprocessing; coherent detection techniques
subnode.content: This paper is mainly devoted to nonrecursive optical preprocessing realizations of the self-coherent principle. Synthetic LO generation is interpreted in terms of estimation theory as a technique for estimating the phase shift keying (DIPPSK) and its contribution by an averaging effect over multiple symbols. The brief differential binary phase-shift keying (DIBPSK) treatments in [2]-[6] are extended to the more complex differential quadrature phase-shift keying (DQPSK) and, more generally, M-array phase-shift keying (M-MSK). In this work, we discuss processing SC architectures and their tradeoffs are compared. In Section II, we review a "polyphase" interleaved version of conventional DPSK. The SC principle is explained in Section III, rigorously modeling the attainable self-coherent gain, i.e., the reduction of phase noise power entailed in the process of synchrotronics. We also present some results on the performance of polyphase operation on esching the DF requirements and provides an overview of the alternative integrated-optoelectronic realizations, which are detailed and compared in Sections VI and VII. For completeness, the electronic postprocessing approach is briefly discussed in Section VIII. The final section concludes with Section IX. Finally, we conclude that the self-coherent versus the digital coherent detection techniques.

subnode.title: II. POLYPHASE DPSK_1
subnode.metadata: polyphase DPSK; differential phase detection; interleaving factor; symbol duration; polyphase rate
subnode.content: In preparation of our treatment of self-coherent DPSK detection, let us first review conventional differential phase detection in a slightly generalized form referred to here as polyphase DPSK (P-DPSK). This variant of DPSK increases the delay in the differential encoder of the DPSK transmitter (TX), as well as in the receiver (RX) processing, from \( \overline{I}_{d} \) to \( \sum_{i} d^{2}T_{d} \), where \( T_{d} \) is the symbol duration (i.e., the symbol or band rate in \( T_{d}^{2} \)), and the called polyphase period, its inverse \( \overline{I}^{*} \) is called the polyphase rate, and \( d \) is an integer called interleaving factor. Possible motivations for conventional P-DPSK [20]-[24] include:

children.title: III. SELF-COHERENT DETECTION PRINCIPLE
children.metadata: DPSK phase estimation; synthetic LO generation; decision feedback reference; phase-noise reduction; multisymbol sliding window; ML estimation theory; coherent LO synthesis; SNR enhancement; D = 3/4/5 chips; 2S-DPSK baseline; noise variance summation; signal amplitude reinforcement; incoherent noise averaging; rotation-based estimation; reference phase improvement; decision error propagation; SNR scaling with D.

subnode.title: III. SELF-COHERENT DETECTION PRINCIPLE_1
subnode.metadata: DF-aided MS-BDPSK; M-ary DPSK; Synthetic LO generation; Phase-noise reduction; Multisymbol sliding window; Receive complexity; DBPSK; DQPSK; DPPS; Two-symbol DPSK
subnode.content: The principle of operation, briefly introduced in [2] for DF-aided MS-BDPSK, is now reviewed and extended to \(M\)-ary DPSK. Synthetic LO generation is interpreted in terms of estimation theory as well as intuitively accounting for it in terms of a phase-noise reduction effect due to averaging independent noise. The proposed approach is based on the assumption that the noise are the phase constellation size \(M\) (in particular, \(M = 2\) for DBPSK and \(M = 4\) for DQPSK) and \(D\), the multisymbol sliding window size in chips, with the fifth roughest chip representing the "signal" while the preceding \(D - 1\) chips, ranging from \(k - D + 1\) to \(k - 1\), are used to synthesize the improved reference (ref). As receive complexity rises upon increasing the number of parameters, we typically consider \(M = 2, 4, 8, \ldots\), i.e., DBPSK, DQPSK, or DPPS. For simplicity, we assume that \(D = 3, 4, 5\) chips. Notice that \(D = 2\) designates conventional DPSK to be henceforth referred to as two-symbol DPSK (2S-DPSK).

subnode.title: III. SELF-COHERENT DETECTION PRINCIPLE_2
subnode.metadata: DPSK noise variance; phase difference decision statistic; PN variance; coherent homodyne PSK; decision feedback; ML estimate; linear channel model; recursive rotation; additive white noise
subnode.content: As discussed in [2] and [3], the noise variance of the DPSK phase difference decision statistic is the sum of PN variances of the two adjacent symbols—the information and reference have been extracted from the PDSK signal. The PDSK is divided by about \(3\mathrm{dB}\) relative to homodyne PSK, as it reduces to \(2.4\mathrm{dB}\) at bit error rate (BER) of \(10^{-3}\) due to the degrading effect of amplitude noise on PSK. In principle, if a precise estimate of the phase of the last symbol reference were available, the DPSK performance would coincide with that of coherent homodyne PSK since the variance of the PN difference would be less than unity. This is not the case for the PDSK, which is considered perfect. By feeding back the previous, mostly correctly decoded, signals, a better phase reference is extracted and effectively used to reduce the PN of the previous (reference) symbol. In essence, the estimate of the phase reference symbol, generated out of previous observations and decision feedback, better than the PN reference symbol, can be obtained by combining the estimation operation. These intuitive statements may be rigorously formalized by applying estimation theory to derive an ML estimate \(\hat{\Delta}_{\mathrm{ML}}^{\mathrm{opt}}\) of the ideal noiseless reference \(\hat{\Delta}_{\mathrm{ML}}^{\mathrm{opt}}\) as a function of the vector of past observations \(\left[\hat{h}_{\mathrm{p - D + 1}},\dots ,\hat{h}_{\mathrm{p - 2}},\hat{h}_{\mathrm{p - 1}}\right]\), assuming the linear channel model \(\hat{h}_{\mathrm{p}} = \hat{A}_{\mathrm{p}}\hat{e}^{i\omega t} + i\hat{k}_{\mathrm{p}}\). children.title: IV. SC DPSK RECEIVERS OVERVIEW
children.metadata: integrated-optoelectronic MZD models; interferometric front-end; soft processor; decision feedback loop; analog postprocessing; multiarm DI devices; polyphase operation; delay-aided signal processing; DC signal conversion; rotation symbol estimation
children.content: We now introduce integrated-optoelectronic realizations of MZD models. The MZD model is a supercell with multiple MZ DIs or more complex multiarm DIs, realizing<|txt_contd|>multiple delays spanning a longer observation window, and subsequent analog soft-processing of these outputs aided by delay.<|txt_split|>At the top level of description, all SC-DPSK receivers compose the following modules with appropriate interconnections: an interferometric front-end (IFE), a soft processor (SP), a slicer, and a decision feedback loop (DFL) (Fig. 2). In the case of DFL, the IFE is composed of a series of interferometric structures than in 2S-DFSK, and the IFE-SP cascade manipulates the received optical signal under the control of the decision feedback relayed from the slicer output via the DFL. The SP generates improved soft-decision sufficient statistics to be quantized by the slicer in order to generate the bit decisions. An advantage of all DF-sided DPSK systems is that the slicer is in fact identical to that of a 2S-DFSK system. This is because the IFE-SP cascade manipulates the transmission device(s) for DBPSK (DOPSK). This is in contrast with MS-DFSK systems without DF [16]-[18], which require considerably more complex slicers. The decision bit(s) provide the information output(s), namely, the estimates \( \hat{h}_{\mathrm{m - 4}}^{\mathrm{b}} \) and for DOPSK also \( \hat{h}_{\mathrm{m - 3}}^{\mathrm{b}} \) of the transmitted bits(s), representing the estimates of the transmitted rotation symbols \( \hat{s}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \). The output bits are tapped to provide the auxiliary output(s) feeding the DFL. Notice the delay of the slicer decisions by one discrete unit due to the inherent one-clock settling period of the DFFs.<|txt_split|>For postprocessing SC-DPSK systems, the IFE coincides with that of 2S-DPSK. In preprocessing schemes, the IFE (Fig. 3) is more complex (but the electronics is simpler), comprising multiple relative delays \( T_{1} \), \( T_{2} \), \( T_{3} \), ..., \( (D - 1)T_{i} \), and \( D \) delay \( T_{i + 1} \). The IFE is a linear combination of a delay value (Sections V-VI) or alternatively realized as integrated multiarm DI devices (Section VII). For systems with<|txt_contd|>\(M > 2\) the DI devices in the IFE come in pairs providing "in-phase" and quadrature optical preprocessing. In the systems of Section IV, the DF is applied to the SP in analog form, which is then converted into a DC signal provided to the IFE, such that the DC degenerates to a simpler form.<|txt_split|>The improved SC DF-aided MS-DSPK receivers are compatible with conventional 25-DSPK transmitters. The only modification possibly required at the TX is the provision of polyphase operation, yielding a beneficial effect on easing the DP delay and reducing the latency. In order to achieve this, they become available one clock cycle later, i.e., \( T_{\mathrm{d}} \) seconds after the "current time" when the noisy decision statistics enter the DFFs in the slicer. Hence, when \( d = 1 \), i.e., without interleaving, the previous estimate of rotation symbol \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) requisite for the generation of the additive term \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} / \tilde{T}_{\mathrm{m - 2}} \) in \( \tilde{R}_{\mathrm{m - 1}}(16) \) must be hauled in zero time from the output of the slicer to the input of the DFF. This is achieved by using the addition \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} / \tilde{T}_{\mathrm{m - 2}} \) beats with \( \tilde{\tau}_{\mathrm{d}} \) at the current time \( k \) to yield the decision statistic entering the slicer. Fortunately, under polyphase operation, the decisions are still generated at the baud rate, yet the reference synthesis occurs at \( d \) times slower polyphase rate. The additive contribution \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} / \tilde{T}_{\mathrm{m - 2}} \) is only required after a delay \( T_{\mathrm{d}} \) relative to the current time, whereas the decision \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) has already become available \( T_{\mathrm{d}} \) seconds after the current time, provided that \( (d - 1)T_{\mathrm{d}} / T_{\mathrm{d}} \) seconds for the loop delay and the forward propagation to the DI output, enabling the transfer of \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) from the slicer output to the point where \( \tilde{\mathcal{P}}_{\mathrm{d}} \tilde{\mathcal{P}}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) enters the slicer input.

The MZD model is a supercell with multiple MZ DIs or more complex multiarm DIs, realizingmultiple delays spanning a longer observation window, and subsequent analog soft-processing of these outputs aided by delay.At the top level of description, all SC-DPSK receivers compose the following modules with appropriate interconnections: an interferometric front-end (IFE), a soft processor (SP), a slicer, and a decision feedback loop (DFL) (Fig. 2). In the case of DFL, the IFE is composed of a series of interferometric structures than in 2S-DFSK, and the IFE-SP cascade manipulates the received optical signal under the control of the decision feedback relayed from the slicer output via the DFL. The SP generates improved soft-decision sufficient statistics to be quantized by the slicer in order to generate the bit decisions. An advantage of all DF-sided DPSK systems is that the slicer is in fact identical to that of a 2S-DFSK system. This is because the IFE-SP cascade manipulates the transmission device(s) for DBPSK (DOPSK). This is in contrast with MS-DFSK systems without DF [16]-[18], which require considerably more complex slicers. The decision bit(s) provide the information output(s), namely, the estimates \( \hat{h}_{\mathrm{m - 4}}^{\mathrm{b}} \) and for DOPSK also \( \hat{h}_{\mathrm{m - 3}}^{\mathrm{b}} \) of the transmitted bits(s), representing the estimates of the transmitted rotation symbols \( \hat{s}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \). subnode.metadata: SC-DPSK postprocessing; 2S-DPSK IFE; multi-delay IFE; DI devices; in-phase/quadrature preprocessing; analog DF to SP; DC signal to IFE
subnode.content: For postprocessing SC-DPSK systems, the IFE coincides with that of 2S-DPSK. In preprocessing schemes, the IFE (Fig. 3) is more complex (but the electronics is simpler), comprising multiple relative delays \( T_{1} \), \( T_{2} \), \( T_{3} \), ..., \( (D - 1)T_{i} \), and \( D \) delay \( T_{i + 1} \). The IFE is a linear combination of a delay value (Sections V-VI) or alternatively realized as integrated multiarm DI devices (Section VII). For systems with\(M > 2\) the DI devices in the IFE come in pairs providing "in-phase" and quadrature optical preprocessing. In the systems of Section IV, the DF is applied to the SP in analog form, which is then converted into a DC signal provided to the IFE, such that the DC degenerates to a simpler form.

subnode.metadata: SC DF-aided MS-DSPK receivers; polyphase operation; DP delay reduction; latency mitigation; noisy decision statistics; DFFs slicer; rotation symbol estimation; baud rate decision; polyphase reference synthesis; additive term delay; loop delay compensation
subnode.content: The improved SC DF-aided MS-DSPK receivers are compatible with conventional 25-DSPK transmitters. The only modification possibly required at the TX is the provision of polyphase operation, yielding a beneficial effect on easing the DP delay and reducing the latency. In order to achieve this, they become available one clock cycle later, i.e., \( T_{\mathrm{d}} \) seconds after the "current time" when the noisy decision statistics enter the DFFs in the slicer. Hence, when \( d = 1 \), i.e., without interleaving, the previous estimate of rotation symbol \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) requisite for the generation of the additive term \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} / \tilde{T}_{\mathrm{m - 2}} \) in \( \tilde{R}_{\mathrm{m - 1}}(16) \) must be hauled in zero time from the output of the slicer to the input of the DFF. This is achieved by using the addition \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} / \tilde{T}_{\mathrm{m - 2}} \) beats with \( \tilde{\tau}_{\mathrm{d}} \) at the current time \( k \) to yield the decision statistic entering the slicer. Fortunately, under polyphase operation, the decisions are still generated at the baud rate, yet the reference synthesis occurs at \( d \) times slower polyphase rate. The additive contribution \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} / \tilde{T}_{\mathrm{m - 2}} \) is only required after a delay \( T_{\mathrm{d}} \) relative to the current time, whereas the decision \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) has already become available \( T_{\mathrm{d}} \) seconds after the current time, provided that \( (d - 1)T_{\mathrm{d}} / T_{\mathrm{d}} \) seconds for the loop delay and the forward propagation to the DI output, enabling the transfer of \( \tilde{\theta}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) from the slicer output to the point where \( \tilde{\mathcal{P}}_{\mathrm{d}} \tilde{\mathcal{P}}_{\mathrm{m - 1}} \) enters the slicer input.

subnode.content: Briefly considering the \(M = 2\) DBPSK format, partially treated in [2]-[4] for special cases of \(\varOmega\), the main simplification relative to DQPSK is that all in-quadrature devices, and associated with a single phase shift, are considered as real-valued or irrelevant statistics. Hence, the treatment essentially becomes real-valued. The rotation symbols are now \(s_k = \pm 1\), a phase tilt is unnecessary, and the CTCs in the SP reduce to the simpler CTCs. (Fig. 7) while the QCRs reduce to XOR gates (Fig. 8). children.metadata: feedback circuitry; phase rotation; analog DFL; SP complexity reduction; time-varying phase modulation; auxiliary DIs; decision feedback; electrooptic processing; MZ DI devices; active electrodes; phase-shift complex factor; decision statistics; IFE structure; switching voltage; DC component; multiplicative phase operations
children.content:
In the next two architectures, the same principle of operation is at work: past reference chips are aligned by the feedback circuitry to add up collinearity, yielding a "cleaner" (higher SNR, lower PN) reference, prior to beating it with the current information. The output of the control circuitry is then used for the detection circuitry is simplified by eliminating the controlled inverters (CIs/CCLs), resulting in a less complex SP at the expense of more complex optical DI devices. All that is left in the SP module is a pair of analog adders (just one adder for DBPSK) used to apply the auxiliary DI outputs as additive corrections to the input signal. This allows the device to be able to reduce expense of resorting to more complex MZ DI devices, incorporating active electrodes. The DFL is now analog rather than digital, consisting of a driver applying band-rate electrical control signal to the active electrodes of the modified DiSs. As the CD/CC1 implementation may be increasingly difficult at higher and higher speeds \((40 / 160\mathrm{Gbps})\) while the electrooptic processing may be more efficient than the conventional DC/CC1, eliminating the CIs/CCLs, may turn out to be a worthwhile one.<|txt_split|>The SP complexity reduction is enabled by the insight that the phase rotations requisite for DF may be generated electrospun. The phase rotation is achieved by a symmetric decision feedback (IDF) feeding the past decisions directly into the DIs in the optical domain rather than into CCIs.<|txt_split|>Inspecting the balanced output of a \(\mathrm{D}[n]\Gamma_{\mathrm{c}}^{-1}\) device (9), and observing that the rotation increments are just phase factors, the idea is to put the phase-shift complex factor \(\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{p}}\) to work in order to realize the mathematical operations of multiplication by \(n\) multiplications. In other words, occurring in the synthesis of the improved decision statistics (37) for \(M\)-ary DPSK with re-im parts expressible as<|txt_split|>\[
\delta_ {k} ^ {\mathrm {e v}} = \sum_ {m = 1} ^ {k - 1} \mathrm {R e} \left\{\delta_ {k} ^ {\mathrm {e v}} \right\} _ {(m - k, m - k + 1)} \mathrm {j} ^ {i \pi / \beta} F _ {k} (\delta_ {k} ^ {\mathrm {e v}}) _ {m - k}
\]<|txt_split|>\[
= \sum_ {m = 1} ^ {n} \mathrm {R a} \left\{\hat {\sigma} ^ {(f _ {m})} \hat {r} _ {m} ^ {*} - r _ {m} \right\} = \sum_ {m = 1} ^ {n} \frac {\hat {\sigma} ^ {(f _ {m})} \hat {r} _ {m} ^ {*}}{m !} [ 3 ] (3 9)
\]<|txt_split|>with \(\frac{\partial^n}{\partial m^m}\equiv \pi /M\wedge \delta_{[m - n - 1],m} = 2,3,\dots ,D - 1\) and similarly for \(\frac{\partial^n}{\partial q^m}\equiv\)<|txt_split|>\[
\hat {Q} _ {n} ^ {e m} = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {2 n} \ln \left\{e ^ {(- i) _ {n} ^ {\prime}} \hat {R} _ {k} ^ {e m} \tau_ {n - m} \right\} = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {2 n} \left\langle q _ {n, T} ^ {(e m)} \right\rangle_ {n} ^ {(m)} [ b ] \tag {40}
\]<|txt_split|>The last form of each variable in (39) and (40) features a summation of outputs of DIs with time-varying bias phases. This suggests an IFE structure containing auxiliary DIs with multiple delays \(T_{i} = T_{i}^{*}Z\), \(\dots, (D - 1)^{i}T_{i}\) wherein the phase biases are no longer constant (as in MZ DIs used in conventional DPSK). In the new structure, time-varying phase modulations<|txt_split|>\[
\gamma_ {k} ^ {(m)}, \gamma_ {k} ^ {(m)} - \pi / 2 \in \{\pi + 2 \pi / \beta \} _ {\beta = 0} ^ {M - 1} / M \tag {41}
\]<|txt_split|>are applied to high-speed electrodes incorporated in each of the auxiliary DIs. Assume a fixed phase bias component \(\pi /M\) is built into the DIs (an alternative would be to generate a DC component of the drive voltage to the modulating electrodes, onto which the DC current is generated). The DC component is superposed. The time-varying modulating drive voltage is then expressed in terms of the switching voltage \(V_{\mathrm{S}}\) as<|txt_split|>\[
V _ {d} [ k ] \equiv \left\{\frac {2 V _ {d} ^ {2} M}{M} \right\} _ {f = 0, n} ^ {M - 1}, \qquad (4 2)
\]

children.content: The image illustrates a three-symbol DQPSK (Differential Quadrature Phase Shift Keying) receiver using a second-class architecture. It features a driver generating the drive voltage v_d[k] with a specific phase sequence, connected to a DFL (Differential Feedback Loop) that includes a 4-level DAC and a 4-bit digital-to-analog converter. The system also includes two DFF (D-type Flip-Flops) and a 4-bit DAC, with outputs labeled as v_out1 and v_out2. The DFL is connected to a 4-level DAC and a 4-bit DAC, and the output signals are processed through a 4-bit DAC and a 4-level DAC. The receiver's operation is based on the electrical balanced output of two in-phase quadrature DPs (Delay Elements) with delays T and 2T, which are summed up and applied to the DFL for phase quadrature decoding.

subnode.title: VII. A THIRD CLASS OF REALIZATIONS_1
subnode.metadata: IDF-driven variant; electropotential synthesis; LDO-defined LO; optical signal pinpointing; reduced IPC count; optical integration complexity
subnode.content: In this section, we introduce a second IDF-driven variant wherein the synthetic LO is directly electropotically synthesized by the LDO. The LDO is defined as an optical signal (e.g., Fig. 12), such that the LO is identifiable as an optical signal at a particular point in the IPC (as opposed to previous schemes in which the LO could not be physically pinpointed but appeared mathematically in the decision statistics at the SP level).From a hardware complexity point of view, in this architecture the collection of multiple DiS is replaced by a smaller number of novel IPCs, which are somewhat more complex. Such level of optical integration is beyond the simple measure of the number of devices that can be integrated on an optical substrate, bearing the potential to further reduce the IPC count and the complexity of devices integrated on each IPC.

children.content: Herefore, we have thoroughly investigated optical preprocessing architectures. Alternative electronic postprocessing SC schemes were introduced in [11]. A simple yet universal digital implementation of the DF SC MS-DPSK receiver architecture was recently proposed for any multilevel DPSK formalism [27]. The optical front-end of the digital self-coherent receiver resembles a single-cell device with a single cell and is designed as required for conventional DQPSK demodulation. Derivations of the data-aided multisymbol phase estimation algorithms for multilevel DPSK and DPSK/amplitude shift keying were also provided for the digital implementation. When additionally including an intensity detection branch, this digital self-coherent receiver is also compatible with the recently introduced techniques reconstructing signal optical field [11], [27], [28], thereby allowing the detection of any multilevel phase and amplitude modulated signal [29] and digital compensation of transimpedance and harmonic dispersion [27], [28], nonlinear phase [30], and polarization-mode dispersion [30] in a similar way as practiced in the recently introduced digital coherent receivers [31]-[38].<|txt_split|>A comparison between the optical preprocessing and electronic postprocessing approaches is carried out in this section, with an emphasis on phase estimation, introducing a related new approach for the analysis of the signal. children.title: Fig. 11. Five-symbol DBPSK \((D = 5, M = 2)\) receiver using the second class of architectures. The DFL now consists of a driver generating three drive voltages \(\{0, V_{\mathrm{R}}\}\) through the logic shown, consisting of XOR gates and delays.
children.metadata: DBPSK, receiver, architecture, XOR, delay, D-FF, DFL
children.content: The image illustrates a five-symbol DBPSK (Differential Binary Phase Shift Keying) receiver using the second class of architectures. It shows five identical input modules, each generating a voltage output (V1[k], V2[k], V3[k]) via logic gates and delays. These outputs are fed into a decision block that determines the symbol decision (b_k-1) and feeds into a D-FF (D-type flip-flop). The D-FF outputs are further processed through an XOR gate and a delay line (DFL) to generate the final output signal. The DFL is shown as a cascaded structure, indicating a feedback path for signal processing. The overall design highlights the use of XOR gates and delays for signal differentiation and synchronization in the receiver.

children.title: Fig. 12. A five-symbol DQPSK receiver using the third class of SC preprocessing architectures, based on a novel DR device with interferometric feedback (Fig. 13). The receiver is split into \(n\) sub-channels, each with a lower arm of the interferometer by summing \(i_{k + 1}\) with phase-rotated versions of \(i_{k + 1} - i_{k - 1} - i_{k - 1}\), where the phase rotations applied by active phase modulation via electrodes led by three electrical transmission lines as shown. The LO signal is then converted to an output voltage in the middle of the transmission directional coupler, followed by balanced photodetection. The drive voltages generated by the DFL driver, corresponding to rotation increments requisite to generate the DC signal, are then used to convert the transmission lines feeding the phase-shifting electrodes in the two devices.

It illustrates a system that processes electrical components by converting them into a digital domain via an analog-to-digital (A/D) conversion. The diagram shows a multi-stage process involving four quadrants of a multiplier (labeled "mult"), which are represented as interconnected blocks with complex-valued connections. subnode.title: VIII. THE ELECTRONIC POSTPROCESSING APPROACH_1
subnode.metadata: optical preprocessing; digital self-coherent receiver; multilevel DPSK; phase estimation; intensity detection; signal reconstruction; nonlinear phase compensation; polarization-mode dispersion correction
subnode.content: Herefore, we have thoroughly investigated optical preprocessing architectures. Alternative electronic postprocessing SC schemes were introduced in [11]. A simple yet universal digital implementation of the DF SC MS-DPSK receiver architecture was recently proposed for any multilevel DPSK formalism [27]. The optical front-end of the digital self-coherent receiver resembles a single-cell device with a single cell and is designed as required for conventional DQPSK demodulation. Derivations of the data-aided multisymbol phase estimation algorithms for multilevel DPSK and DPSK/amplitude shift keying were also provided for the digital implementation. When additionally including an intensity detection branch, this digital self-coherent receiver is also compatible with the recently introduced techniques reconstructing signal optical field [11], [27], [28], thereby allowing the detection of any multilevel phase and amplitude modulated signal [29] and digital compensation of transimpedance and harmonic dispersion [27], [28], nonlinear phase [30], and polarization-mode dispersion [30] in a similar way as practiced in the recently introduced digital coherent receivers [31]-[38].

children.title: IX.DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS
children.metadata: self-coherent detection; optical preprocessing; electronic postprocessing; digital coherent detection; attribute comparison
children.content: This paper was devoted to explaining the self-coherent detection principle, introducing and comparing several realizations based on the optical preprocessing and electronic postprocessing approaches.<|txt_split|>Below we compare the main attributes of the self-coherent detection versus those of the emerging digital coherent detection.

children.title: A. Self-Coherent Detection-Pros
children.metadata: Optical detection without local oscillator; High sensitivity comparable to coherent detection; Polarization-independent detection; Multilevel DPSK detection; Chromatic dispersion compensation
children.content: - Eliminates the optical local oscillator and optical phaselocked loop required in coherent detection.<|txt_split|>- Can provide sensitivity approaching coherent detection.<|txt_split|>- Might not require polarization control or diversity (provided polarization independent DIs are used and polarization multiplexing is not used at the transmitter, i.e., a unique polarization state is being modulated).<|txt_split|>- Allows for the detection of multilevel DPSK signals [26], [29].<|txt_split|>- May allow for the digital compensation of chromatic dispersion in a single phase noise [30], and polarization-mode dispersion [30].

children.title: B. Self-Coherent Detection—Cons
children.metadata: High analog-to-digital resolution; Enhanced digital signal processing; Optical filtering for noise rejection; High-quality components in wide-length division multiplexing systems
children.content:
- May require higher analog-to-digital conversion resolution and more digital signal-processing power than a digital converter.<|txt_split|>- Requires optical filtering to reject noise and adjacent channel noise.  
- Requires the use of a high-quality (issue in most wide-length division multiplexing systems).

children.title: C. Digital Coherent Detection [31]-[38]Pros
children.metadata: digital signal reconstruction; wavelength selectivity; high receiver sensitivity; arbitrary I&Q modulation; research challenges; noise analysis; BER performance; pre- and postprocessing schemes; complexity optimization; hard-detection SC systems
children.content: - Allows the digital reconstruction of the signal field, permitting compensation of various transmission impairments such as chromatic and polarization-mode dispersion and nonlinear phase noise.<|txt_split|>- Provides wavelength selectivity through RF and digital filters rather than optical filters.<|txt_split|>- Provides the highest receiver sensitivity.<|txt_split|>- Applicable to arbitrary I&Q, not necessarily differential, modulated formats (PSK, QAM, etc.).<|txt_split|>Research challenges still outstanding are the evaluation of the quality of the research and the need to improve the work aiming at additional reduction of realization complexity.<|txt_split|>The precise noise analysis and error probability performance of the pre- and postprocessing self-coherent scheme is deferred to a future publication. The BER analysis will account for three key impairments affecting differential phase transmission over a fiber-optic link, namely, nonlinear SPM-induced phase noise, nonlinearity in the frequency domain, and noise in the spectrum. It will be seen that the two schemes (pre- versus postprocessing) have nearly equivalent performance, both approaching that of coherent detection. Hence, the selection of one scheme versus<|txt_contd|>the other ought to be driven by complexity-of-realization considerations, e.g., the complexity of integrated-optic front-end and analog electronics in the preprocessing case, versus the availability of high-speed analog-to-digital converters and the complexity of the digital signal processors in the postprocessing case.<|txt_split|>A related promising research direction concerns a class of SC realizations based on hard-detection [5], [39].

subnode.content: - Allows the digital reconstruction of the signal field, permitting compensation of various transmission impairments such as chromatic and polarization-mode dispersion and nonlinear phase noise.- Provides wavelength selectivity through RF and digital filters rather than optical filters.- Provides the highest receiver sensitivity.- Applicable to arbitrary I&Q, not necessarily differential, modulated formats (PSK, QAM, etc.).Research challenges still outstanding are the evaluation of the quality of the research and the need to improve the work aiming at additional reduction of realization complexity.

subnode.title: Self-Coherent Multisymbol Detection of Optical Differential Phase-Shift Keying_1
subnode.metadata: self-coherent detection; multivector decision-feedback; optical preprocessing; electronic postprocessing; synthetic local oscillator; DPSK; integrated-optoelectronic receiver; digital coherent detection
subnode.content: Moshe Nazarathy, Senior Member, IEEE, Xiang Liu, Senior Member, IEEE, Louis Christen, Member IEEE, Yannick K. Lize, Student Member, IEEE, Student Member, OSA, and Alan E. Willner, Fellow, IEEE, Fellow, OSAAbstract—This paper treats the recently emerging self-coherent multivector decision-feedback-aided detection techniques for optoelectronic devices. The goal of this work is to investigate both an optical preprocessing approach and an electronic postprocessing approach. We present in detail (i) the generation of a synthetic low-phase-noise effective local oscillator out of the modulation of the electric field, and (ii) the application of the hybrid (i) and (ii) the role of polypeptide (interleaved) DPSK in the optical preprocessing approach in easing the decision feedback realization process. In particular, we show that the proposed method can be used for general \(M_{\mathrm{f}}\) and differential quadrature phase-shift keying in particular; iv) three alternative integrated-optoelectronic receiver implementations for the optical preprocessing approach detailing the different processing strategies, including digital and electronic analog and digital building blocks; and (v) the principle, and alternative implementations, of the digital postprocessing approach to self-coherent detection. We finally compare the capabilities of the self-coherent detection and digital coherent detection techniques.
